food_name: Barley Water, Traditional Singapore Style (300 ml, sweetened with rock sugar)
region_of_origin: Southeast Asia; ubiquitous in Singapore at hawker centres, kopitiams, supermarkets (bottled), and home kitchens. Rooted in Chinese herbal drink tradition brought by Hokkien and Cantonese immigrants.
food_subgroup: Traditional grain-based sweetened drink; barley water boiled from pearl barley or Job's tears (Coix lacryma-jobi) with pandan leaves and rock sugar, served hot or cold at 300 ml per cup.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for prepared sweetened barley water as a beverage. Closest parent would be a grain-based beverage; no exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Barley-based drink (closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0P0D used as closest defensible parent for a prepared barley beverage. No exact code exists for sweetened barley water.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for traditional sweetened barley water beverage.

main_ingredients: pearl barley or Job's tears (Coix lacryma-jobi)
allergens: gluten (if pearl barley is used; Job's tears variant is gluten-free)

serving.description: 1 cup = 300 ml (approximately 300 g including suspended cooked barley grains)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one cup of 300 ml (approximately 300 g), as sold at Singapore hawker centres or poured from a home-prepared pot.

nutrient_source.title: Barley Water — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database entry for barley water (sweetened, traditional preparation). Energy 30 kcal/100 g, carbohydrate 7.5 g/100 g, sugar 6.8 g/100 g, protein 0.1 g/100 g, fat 0.0 g/100 g confirmed from HPB record. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by HPB for this entry; -1 used to indicate not reported rather than zero.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Barley-Based Beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for traditional sweetened barley water
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for traditional Singapore sweetened barley water. GI of approximately 45 (range 40–50) is estimated using the closest analogous entry for barley-based beverages. Rock sugar (sucrose equivalent, GI ~65) is the primary sweetener; the GI is blunted by the drink matrix effect and trace beta-glucan from barley. GL per 300 ml serving = (GI 45 × available carbohydrate 22.5 g) / 100 ≈ 10, rounded to 9 based on moderate sweetness assumption consistent with HPB data. Commercial bottled variants (e.g. Yeo's, Pokka) carry higher sugar loads (GL 14–20 per 300 ml) and should be treated separately.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 2 mg per 100 g; approximately 6 mg per 300 ml serving
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is negligible at approximately 6 mg per 300 ml serving; this drink poses no meaningful hypertensive risk, though the added rock sugar (approximately 20 g per 300 ml) should be considered in the context of overall metabolic health for patients with hypertension-associated metabolic syndrome.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI approximately 45, GL approximately 9 per 300 ml (home/hawker); GL 14–20 for commercial bottled variants
type2_diabetes.guidance: Traditional home or hawker-prepared barley water has a GI of approximately 45 and a GL of approximately 9 per 300 ml, which is manageable for most people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; however, commercial bottled barley water (Yeo's, Pokka) contains 30–35 g sugar per 300 ml (GL 14–20) and should be avoided or strictly limited, and patients should be counselled that the traditional 'cooling health drink' perception often leads to underestimation of sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.0 g per 100 g; no cholesterol; low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Barley water contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and negligible sodium, making it cardiovascularly neutral as a beverage; the primary cardiovascular consideration is the cumulative sugar load from rock sugar sweetening, which should be minimised in patients with dyslipidaemia or metabolic syndrome.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Barley Water (薏米水, Yì mǐ shuǐ) is a traditional Chinese-origin herbal beverage that is ubiquitous in Singapore at hawker centres, kopitiams, and supermarkets. It is prepared by boiling pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare) or Job's tears (Coix lacryma-jobi, also called Chinese barley or 薏米) in water with pandan leaves until the liquid becomes slightly cloudy and viscous, then sweetening with rock sugar. The drink is served hot or cold in approximately 300 ml portions. Nutritionally, a 300 ml serving of the traditional moderately sweetened preparation provides approximately 90 kcal, 22.5 g carbohydrate (of which approximately 20 g is from rock sugar), 0.3 g protein, and negligible fat. GI is approximately 45 (low category), GL approximately 9 per serving. IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: Pearl barley contains gluten and is unsafe for coeliac disease; Job's tears (薏米) is gluten-free. Commercial bottled variants (Yeo's, Pokka) typically contain 30–35 g sugar per 300 ml (GL 14–20) and are nutritionally distinct from home or hawker preparations. Patients wishing to reduce sugar should request 'siu tang' (少糖, less sugar) or 'wu tang' (無糖, no sugar). Rock sugar is sucrose and carries the same GI as white sugar despite its traditional health associations. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo database. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: is barley water good for diabetes
barley water vs job's tears difference

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework as no direct entry exists for this specific preparation. Micronutrient values for monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported by HPB for this entry and are marked -1. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: Barley Water (薏米水) is one of the most commonly consumed traditional herbal beverages in Singapore. Two botanically distinct grains are used interchangeably under the same name: pearl barley (Hordeum vulgare, contains gluten) and Job's tears (Coix lacryma-jobi, gluten-free). This distinction is clinically important for coeliac patients. The HPB Foodo database entry used here reflects a moderately sweetened traditional preparation. Commercial bottled versions (Yeo's, Pokka) are nutritionally distinct with higher sugar content and should be recorded separately. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the HPB source for this entry; -1 values indicate data not reported rather than confirmed zero. The GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI study on Singapore-style sweetened barley water has not been identified in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date.
